narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we could responsibly support—it's not about you as a person, but the patterns it spotted in your profile that echoed too many past struggles. At the top of what weighed heaviest was your Year 3 decile of 1.0, placing you in the bottom tenth of your school that year, which was the strongest signal pulling toward potential failure, as students in that spot have historically faced steep climbs to pass the bar. Right alongside that, your Year 1 decile of 1.0 doubled down on that concern, showing this wasn't a one-off but a consistent early challenge in keeping up academically. Your LSAT score of 20.0 added to the picture, as lower scores like yours have often correlated with tougher bar prep hurdles in our data. Coming from a lower-middle family income bracket factored in too, since it sometimes means fewer resources for the intense studying the bar demands. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.0, while solid, didn't quite counterbalance those trends, nudging the outlook downward. Even elements like being a white female pursuing full-time law studies played small roles in the model's view, aligning with patterns where similar profiles have higher failure odds. Together, these painted a story of someone starting from the back of the pack in key academic measures, and with the 59.9% predicted chance of not passing the bar, we had to make a tough call to protect our program's outcomes. But here's the hopeful part—this isn't destiny; many have turned around similar starts by targeting those early grades or boosting test prep. You have the drive we saw in your target for law school, and with focused steps on academics and resources, you could rewrite this story elsewhere. We're rooting for you to prove the model wrong. Keep pushing forward.
